race.name: Gran Fondo Il Lombardia Felice Gimondi
race.tagline: The Classic of the Falling Leaves. One Monument. Two legendary climbs. Lake Como in autumn. Ride the roads that close cycling's greatest season.

vitals.date: Second Sunday of October annually (day before the professional Il Lombardia)
terrain_types: Technical descents on narrow Italian roads
vitals.start_time: 7:30 AM from Corso Europa, Cantu (grids open 6:30 AM)
vitals.registration: Online via ENDU.net. Registration typically opens in spring, closes one week before race day. Credit card, bank transfer, or Satispay accepted.
vitals.prize_purse: Age-group podium awards. UCI Gran Fondo qualifying event — top finishers earn qualification for UCI Gran Fondo World Championships.
vitals.aid_stations: 3 feed zones along the route plus start/finish area support. Medical assistance, breakdown service, and broom wagon throughout.
distance_options: Gran Fondo: 109.5 km / 68 mi with 1,700m climbing — full route including Muro di Sormano and Madonna del Ghisallo
Alternative Sormano bypass: 2 km detour avoids the Muro di Sormano via a milder gradient to the same summit
vitals.entry_fee: EUR 75-120 (tiered pricing, early registration discounts). Includes race number, Castelli jersey, race bag, chip timing, feed zones, medical assistance, breakdown service, broom wagon, and finisher medal.

climate.description: Mid-October in the Lake Como foothills means cool, crisp autumn conditions. Temperatures typically range from 8-16C (47-61F), with morning starts in the low single digits possible. The famous autumn foliage that gives the professional race its nickname — La Classica delle Foglie Morte, the Classic of the Falling Leaves — is at its peak. Rainfall probability is moderate (roughly 12 rain days in October for the Como area), and when it comes, the steep descents off the Ghisallo and Sormano become treacherous. Fog in the valleys is possible in the early morning but usually burns off.
challenges: Cool morning start temperatures (potentially 5-8C at 7:30 AM in October)
Moderate rain probability — wet descents off Sormano and Ghisallo are serious hazards
Temperature differentials between valleys and climb summits
Fallen leaves on descent roads — reduced grip on corners
Potential valley fog at the early morning start

terrain.primary: Paved — Italian provincial and municipal roads
terrain.surface: Well-maintained Italian asphalt throughout. The legendary climbs are on narrow but paved Italian mountain roads. Descents are technical with tight hairpins, particularly off the Ghisallo. The Brianza hills in the opening 25 km are rolling and scenic. Road surfaces vary from excellent on main roads to rough-but-rideable on the steeper mountain sections. Expect Italian road furniture — narrow lanes, stone walls close to the road, occasional rough patches.
features: Muro di Sormano: 1.9 km at 15.8% average, maximum 27% — one of the steepest paved roads in European cycling
Colma di Sormano: 5.1 km at 6.6% average leading into the Muro
Madonna del Ghisallo: 8.5 km at 6.2% average, maximum 14% — home of the cycling patron saint
Rolling Brianza hills for the opening and closing 25 km
Technical descents on narrow Italian mountain roads with hairpin bends
Nearly 20 km descent from Ghisallo summit to Merone

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Monument sportive. The amateur version of Il Lombardia — the last of cycling's five Monuments, run on the same roads the day before the professional race. Organized by RCS Sport (Giro d'Italia organizers). Named for Felice Gimondi, two-time Il Lombardia champion. UCI-sanctioned with chip timing and age-group competition. Prestige=5 as a Monument sportive places a T2 floor on the tier.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: prestige=5 for Monument status — Il Lombardia is one of cycling's five sacred one-day races, first run in 1905. experience=5 for the combination of the Muro di Sormano (27% max gradient), Madonna del Ghisallo (cycling's patron saint), Lake Como scenery, and autumn foliage on Monument roads. elevation=4 for 1,700m of climbing compressed into 109.5 km with the brutally steep Sormano. adventure=4 for the Lake Como setting and the emotional weight of riding Monument roads in the falling leaves. cultural_impact=4 for the Gimondi tribute, 120 years of Il Lombardia history, and the patron saint of cycling at the Ghisallo. altitude=1 because maximum elevation is only ~1,100m — no meaningful altitude stress.

logistics.explanation: Cantu is approximately 40 minutes from Milan Malpensa (MXP), one of Italy's best-connected international airports. Milan Linate and Bergamo Orio al Serio are also within an hour. That's excellent airport access by European sportive standards. The complication is Cantu itself — a small Italian town, not a tourist destination, with limited accommodation within walking distance of the start. Most riders stay in Como (20 minutes by car) or use organized tour operator packages. Packet pickup is in Cantu the day before. Parking exists but is constrained. Italian small-town logistics apply: charming but not always efficient.

length.explanation: At 109.5 km with 1,700m of climbing, this is a proper Gran Fondo but not an ultra-distance event. Winning times around 3 hours suggest a fierce racing tempo for the front of the field. For mid-pack riders, expect 4-5 hours. The route is brutally front-loaded — the Muro di Sormano hits within the first 35 km — and the return through the Brianza hills is a war of attrition on tired legs. The distance is manageable for any trained cyclist, but the climbing density makes it ride longer than the numbers suggest.

technicality.explanation: Fully paved, well-organized Italian roads. The technical demands come from the descents, not the surface. The drop off the Ghisallo summit is fast, narrow, and hairpin-laden — in the wet, it requires genuine bike handling skill. The Muro di Sormano's 27% maximum gradient demands extreme low-gear climbing technique. But this is a road race on roads. No gravel, no off-camber, no navigation. The Italian road furniture — narrow lanes, stone walls, occasional rough patches — adds texture but not real technical challenge.

elevation.explanation: 1,700 meters of climbing in 109.5 km is a serious ratio — nearly 16m of climbing per horizontal kilometer. But the numbers understate the reality. The Muro di Sormano is 1.9 km at 15.8% average with a 27% maximum gradient. That is one of the steepest paved roads any cycling event uses anywhere in the world. It is the kind of gradient that makes strong cyclists walk. After the Sormano, the Ghisallo is 8.5 km at 6.2% — a long, grinding Classics-style climb. Then the final 20 km back to Cantu is rolling with short, sharp climbs that punish tired legs. The aggregate elevation is not Dolomite-level, but the intensity is savage.

climate.explanation: Mid-October in the Lake Como foothills is generally benign for cycling. Temperatures in the 8-16C range are comfortable for racing efforts. The main risk is rain — the Como area averages 12 rain days in October, and when it rains, the steep descents become genuinely dangerous. Fallen autumn leaves on road surfaces add a slippery layer in corners. But on a dry, clear October day, this is some of the most beautiful cycling weather on Earth: golden light, turning leaves, cool air, mountain views. Climate is unlikely to be the defining challenge unless you're unlucky.

altitude.explanation: Maximum elevation on the course is approximately 1,100m at the Colma di Sormano / Madonna del Ghisallo. This is well below any altitude that would affect performance or require acclimatization. The climbs are steep but low. No altitude consideration is needed for this race.

adventure.explanation: Lake Como in autumn is one of the most beautiful cycling settings on the planet. The route passes through the rolling Brianza hills, climbs through chestnut forests to the Ghisallo sanctuary, skirts the shores of Lake Como with views of the villas and mountains, and tackles the infamous Muro di Sormano — a road so steep it was removed from the professional race for decades because it was considered inhumane. The autumn foliage that gives the race its nickname — the Classic of the Falling Leaves — is at its peak. The emotional weight of riding roads that have hosted the Monument since 1905 adds a dimension that pure scenery cannot. This is not a wilderness adventure, but it is a cycling pilgrimage.

prestige.explanation: Il Lombardia is one of cycling's five Monuments — the most prestigious one-day races in the sport, alongside Milan-San Remo, Tour of Flanders, Paris-Roubaix, and Liege-Bastogne-Liege. The professional race has been held since 1905. The Gran Fondo is organized by RCS Sport, the same organization that runs the Giro d'Italia, and it takes place on the same roads the day before the professional Monument. It is named for Felice Gimondi — winner of all three Grand Tours, two-time Il Lombardia champion, and one of the greatest Italian cyclists in history. The Madonna del Ghisallo, on the course, is the patron saint of cycling. There is no higher prestige ceiling for a road cycling sportive.

race_quality.explanation: RCS Sport knows how to organize cycling events — they run the Giro d'Italia, Milan-San Remo, Strade Bianche, and the professional Il Lombardia. This expertise shows. UCI-sanctioned with chip timing and official age-group rankings. The entry fee includes a Castelli jersey (not a cheap race tech tee), professional timing, three feed zones, medical support, broom wagon, and finisher medal. Closed roads on the climbs. The event is a qualifying race for the UCI Gran Fondo World Championships. The only knock: it's a relatively young event (first edition 2019), so the organizational refinement is still developing compared to decades-old sportives.

experience.explanation: The Muro di Sormano at 27% maximum gradient. The Madonna del Ghisallo and the church of the patron saint of cycling at the summit. Lake Como glittering below as you descend toward Bellagio. Autumn leaves falling on roads that Coppi, Merckx, Gimondi, and Pogacar have raced. The knowledge that tomorrow, the professional peloton will ride these same roads in one of cycling's most hallowed Monuments. RCS organizes the event with Italian flair — espresso at the start, pasta party at the finish, the roar of Italian fans on the climbs. For any cyclist who understands the history and romance of European road racing, this is a bucket-list experience. The Muro di Sormano alone — attempting a 27% gradient on roads that were removed from the professional race for being too cruel — is an experience you will never forget.

community.explanation: Around 2,000-2,500 riders from 25+ nations, predominantly Italian with a strong British and Northern European contingent. The atmosphere is competitive Italian gran fondo — riders racing for UCI age-group positions, teams in matching kit, the seriousness of European amateur racing. This is not the casual, community-festival atmosphere of an American gravel event. The Italian fans on the climbs are loud and passionate. The pasta party in Cantu's Piazza Marconi is convivial. But the community is that of competitive European amateur cycling, not an inclusive social gathering.

field_depth.explanation: UCI sanctioning and World Championship qualifying attract strong age-group riders, particularly Italian ex-professionals and serious amateur racers. The front of the field is genuinely fast — winning times around 3 hours for 110 km with 1,700m climbing indicate category 1/2 level fitness. The age-group competition is the real race-within-the-race. But the total field of 2,000-2,500 is modest compared to larger European sportives, and the event's relative youth (since 2019) means it hasn't yet built the depth of decades-old gran fondos like the Maratona dles Dolomites.

value.explanation: The entry fee (approximately EUR 75-120 depending on registration timing) includes a Castelli jersey, chip timing, feed zones, medical support, broom wagon, race bag, and finisher medal. A Castelli jersey alone retails for EUR 80+, so the tangible value is strong. UCI sanctioning and World Championship qualifying add competitive value. The RCS Sport organizational quality is high. For what you receive — Monument roads, professional organization, quality jersey, competitive structure — this is fair value for a European sportive. Not cheap, but not overpriced.

expenses.explanation: Getting to Cantu requires a flight to Milan (well-served by budget airlines from across Europe) plus 40-60 minutes of ground transfer. Accommodation in Como runs EUR 100-200/night for a decent hotel — October is shoulder season, so not peak pricing. Budget EUR 50-80/day for Italian food. Tour operator packages (Sportive Breaks, Cycle Classic Tours, etc.) bundle flights, hotels, transfers, and entry for GBP 500-700. Total budget for a UK/European rider: EUR 800-1,500 for a long weekend including flights, 2-3 nights accommodation, food, and entry. Reasonable for a Monument sportive in northern Italy.

biased_opinion.summary: There are five Monuments in professional cycling. Five races that define the sport's soul. Il Lombardia is the last of them — the autumn closer, the Classic of the Falling Leaves, the race that has crowned the season's strongest one-day rider since 1905. And now you can ride it. The Gran Fondo Il Lombardia puts you on the Muro di Sormano — 27% gradient, the road so brutal it was removed from the professional race for three decades. It sends you up the Madonna del Ghisallo, past the church of cycling's patron saint, with Lake Como spread out below like a postcard that became reality. It wraps you in the autumn colors that gave the race its name. RCS Sport — the people who run the Giro d'Italia — handle the organization. Felice Gimondi's name is on the event. And the day after you finish, you can stand on the same roads and watch Pogacar, van der Poel, and Evenepoel race the Monument itself. This is as close as amateur cycling gets to the sacred.
strengths: Monument pedigree: the amateur version of one of cycling's five most prestigious one-day races, organized by RCS Sport
Muro di Sormano: 1.9 km at 27% maximum gradient — one of the steepest paved roads in European cycling, unforgettable suffering
Madonna del Ghisallo: the patron saint of cycling, the chapel at the summit, Lake Como views — cycling's most spiritual climb
Autumn Lake Como: golden light, falling leaves, mountain views — peak 'Classic of the Falling Leaves' atmosphere
UCI-sanctioned with World Championship qualifying — this is real racing, not just a ride
weaknesses: Young event (since 2019) — still building the depth and traditions of established Italian gran fondos
Cantu is functional, not glamorous — accommodation logistics require staying in Como and transferring
October weather risk: rain transforms the steep descents from challenging to genuinely dangerous
Moderate field size (~2,000) means less festival atmosphere than larger sportives
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you have ever watched Il Lombardia on television and wondered what the Muro di Sormano actually feels like at 27%, this is how you find out. Monument roads. Autumn leaves. Lake Como. The patron saint of cycling. Ride like a hero.

final_verdict.one_liner: The last Monument of the season — now yours to ride. Muro di Sormano at 27%. Madonna del Ghisallo. Lake Como in the falling leaves.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to experience a cycling Monument from the inside — the same climbs, the same roads, the same autumn atmosphere that has defined professional cycling's season finale since 1905. Yes if the Muro di Sormano's 27% gradient is the kind of challenge that excites rather than terrifies you. Yes if UCI age-group competition and World Championship qualifying matter to your cycling ambitions. Skip if you want a pure gravel or off-road challenge. Skip if the October rain risk in northern Italy concerns you. Skip if you prefer large, festival-atmosphere events over the focused intensity of a competitive Italian gran fondo.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want another Italian Monument sportive: try Gran Fondo Strade Bianche for Tuscan white roads and Siena's Piazza del Campo. If you want a massive Italian gran fondo with Dolomite climbing: try the Maratona dles Dolomites. If you want the ultimate Alpine road challenge: try La Marmotte with 5,000m+ of climbing and a summit finish on Alpe d'Huez. If you want the Tour de France experience: try L'Etape du Tour.

logistics.airport: Milan Malpensa (MXP) — 50 km, ~40 minutes to Cantu. Milan Linate (LIN) — 55 km, ~50 minutes. Bergamo Orio al Serio (BGY) — 65 km, ~55 minutes (budget airline hub). Group airport transfers available via tour operators.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Como (20 minutes from Cantu start) for the widest selection of hotels, restaurants, and atmosphere. Lake Como in October is shoulder season — hotel pricing is reasonable compared to summer. Alternatively, stay in Cantu itself for proximity to the start, though options are limited. Tour operator packages (Sportive Breaks, Cycle Classic Tours, Italy Bike Tours) bundle accommodation, transfers, and race entry. Book early — Monument weekend fills up.
logistics.food: Como and the surrounding Lake Como towns offer outstanding Italian cuisine — risotto alla Milanese, polenta with lake fish (missoltini), pizzoccheri. Pre-race: carb-load at any of Como's excellent trattorias. Post-race: pasta party in Piazza Marconi, Cantu. The Lake Como food scene alone justifies the trip.
logistics.packet_pickup: Expo and packet pickup in Cantu the day before (Saturday). Collect race number, Castelli jersey, and race bag. The expo area is on Corso Europa near the start/finish.
logistics.parking: Limited parking in central Cantu — arrive early. Designated parking areas are communicated pre-race. Many riders use organized transfers from Como hotels. The early 6:30 AM grid opening means parking fills fast.

history.founder: RCS Sport (Giro d'Italia organizers) in partnership with the Gimondi family
history.origin_story: Il Lombardia — the professional Monument — has been racing since 1905, when journalist Tullo Morgagni and La Gazzetta dello Sport organized a new race called Milano-Milano. For over a century, the Lake Como climbs belonged exclusively to the professionals. That changed in 2019 when RCS Sport finally created a proper Gran Fondo on the Monument's roads, replacing the previous Gran Fondo Guanella that had occupied the same weekend. The inaugural Gran Fondo Il Lombardia Enel drew 2,500 international riders to tackle the Ghisallo and Sormano. In 2020, the event was named for Felice Gimondi — the Bergamo-born champion who won Il Lombardia in 1966 and 1973, won all three Grand Tours, and the 1973 World Championship before his death in August 2019. The Gimondi family's blessing transformed the event from a sportive into a tribute.
notable_moments: 2019: Inaugural edition as Gran Fondo Il Lombardia Enel — 2,500 riders. Davide Busuito wins in 3h 00m 21s
2019: Named in honor of Felice Gimondi following the champion's death in August 2019
2024: Over 2,000 riders. Giuseppe Orlando wins men's race in 2h 57m 08s. Laura Simenc wins women's race in 3h 16m 47s
The Muro di Sormano — removed from the professional Il Lombardia in 1962 for being too steep, reinstated in 2012 — becomes the Gran Fondo's defining moment
history.reputation: The newest of the Monument sportives, but carrying 120 years of Il Lombardia heritage. The race that proves you don't need decades of gran fondo tradition when you have the Muro di Sormano at 27% and the patron saint of cycling at the Ghisallo summit.

course_description.character: A race of two legendary climbs bookended by the rolling Brianza hills. The route starts and finishes in Cantu, diving immediately into the undulating roads of the Brianza — the gentle, green foothills south of Lake Como that lull you into thinking this will be manageable. It is not. Within 30 km, the road tilts upward toward the Colma di Sormano, and then the gradient goes vertical: the Muro di Sormano, 1.9 km at 15.8% average with ramps touching 27%. After the Muro's savage descent, the route winds along Lake Como toward the second act: the Madonna del Ghisallo, 8.5 km of steady climbing to the church of cycling's patron saint. A sweeping 20 km descent to Merone gives temporary respite before the final 20 km of short, sharp climbs back to Cantu destroy whatever is left in your legs. The autumn colors, the lake views, the chapel at the Ghisallo summit — this course is as beautiful as it is brutal.

suffering_zones.label: Brianza Hills
suffering_zones.desc: The opening 25 km through the Brianza — constant undulations on the roads south of Lake Como. Rolling terrain that never flattens out, slowly accumulating fatigue before the real climbing begins. The autumn foliage in the Brianza is stunning: chestnut trees, golden light, narrow Italian roads lined with stone walls.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling hills, 2-4% gradients, paved Italian provincial roads
suffering_zones.named_section: Brianza — the foothills of Lake Como

suffering_zones.label: Colma di Sormano
suffering_zones.desc: The approach to the main event. 5.1 km at 6.6% average — a proper, grinding climb through the forest that softens you up for what comes next. The gradient is honest and sustained. Experienced riders know this is just the appetizer.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sustained 6.6% average gradient, forested road, narrowing as it climbs

suffering_zones.label: Muro di Sormano
suffering_zones.desc: The wall. 1.9 km at 15.8% average with a maximum gradient of 27%. This is one of the steepest paved roads used in any cycling event on Earth. The road was removed from the professional Il Lombardia in 1962 because it was considered inhumane — riders were walking, and the race organization couldn't justify it. It was reinstated in 2012. On the Gran Fondo, the Muro is the moment of truth: you either ride it or you walk it. At 27%, most riders are in their lowest gear, weaving across the road, heart rate at maximum, legs screaming. There is an alternative 2 km bypass for those who choose discretion over valor.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 15.8% average, 27% maximum, concrete sections, brutally steep hairpins

suffering_zones.label: Madonna del Ghisallo
suffering_zones.desc: The spiritual heart of the race. 8.5 km at 6.2% average with a maximum of 14% near the summit. The climb rises from the shores of Lake Como through forest switchbacks to the chapel of the Madonna del Ghisallo — the patron saint of cycling, recognized by Pope Pius XII in 1949. The church at the summit houses jerseys, bikes, and trophies from cycling's greatest champions. The views of Lake Como from the upper slopes are breathtaking. After the Muro's violence, the Ghisallo is almost meditative — still hard, but beautiful.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 8.5 km at 6.2% average, maximum 14%, paved mountain road with hairpins
suffering_zones.named_section: Madonna del Ghisallo — Cycling's Sacred Climb

suffering_zones.label: Ghisallo Descent to Merone
suffering_zones.desc: Nearly 20 km of descent from the Ghisallo summit to Merone in the valley. Fast, technical, with tight hairpins and narrow Italian roads. In dry conditions, this is exhilarating. In the wet, it is treacherous. Tired legs and cold hands make the technical demands real. This descent is where the race is won and lost in the age-group competition — the brave descenders gain minutes.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Technical descent, hairpin bends, narrow roads, ~20 km

suffering_zones.label: Brianza Attrition — The Final 20 km
suffering_zones.desc: The route back to Cantu through the Brianza hills is a war of attrition. Short, sharp climbs — none individually fearsome, but collectively devastating on legs that have already conquered the Muro and the Ghisallo. The terrain never flattens. Every false flat is actually uphill. The final kilometers into Cantu are a test of how much you have left. The answer, for most, is not much.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling hills with short steep ramps, 3-8% gradients, cumulative fatigue terrain
suffering_zones.named_section: Brianza finale — the war of attrition

course_description.signature_challenge: The Muro di Sormano. 1.9 kilometers at 27% maximum gradient. The road that was removed from the professional Monument for being too cruel. The road that was reinstated because cycling realized it needed places where the strongest riders separate from the merely strong. On the Gran Fondo, the Muro is where your training, your gearing, your body weight, and your willingness to suffer converge into a single, vertical question: can you ride this wall?

guide_variables.race_name: Gran Fondo Il Lombardia Felice Gimondi
guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Italian roads — brutal climbs (Muro di Sormano 27%, Ghisallo 14%), rolling Brianza hills, technical descents
guide_variables.race_weather: Autumn Lake Como: 8-16C (47-61F), moderate rain risk, cool mornings, potential fallen leaves on descents
race_challenges: Muro di Sormano: 1.9 km at 27% maximum gradient — extreme steep climbing
Madonna del Ghisallo: 8.5 km at 6.2% average — long sustained climbing
1,700m of climbing compressed into 109.5 km — high climbing density
Technical descents on narrow Italian roads — bike handling under fatigue
Rolling Brianza hills in the final 20 km — cumulative attrition on tired legs
October weather variability — potential rain makes steep descents hazardous

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained steep climbing — repeated intervals at 12-20% gradient to simulate the Muro di Sormano
non_negotiables.why: The Muro di Sormano's 27% maximum gradient is not something you can muscle through on race day without specific preparation. Train on the steepest hills available, in your lowest gear, at cadences below 60 RPM. If your local terrain doesn't offer 15%+ gradients, use a turbo trainer at maximum resistance. The goal is to teach your legs and cardiovascular system to produce power at extreme low cadence under high torque.

non_negotiables.requirement: Back-to-back climb simulation — 2+ hours of continuous climbing with varied gradients
non_negotiables.why: The Colma di Sormano, Muro di Sormano, and Madonna del Ghisallo come in sequence with only partial recovery between them. Your long ride should include 60-90 minutes of climbing at 5-7% (simulating the Ghisallo), followed by a recovery descent, then repeated steep efforts at maximum gradient (simulating the Muro). The final 20 km of rolling Brianza hills require climbing on pre-fatigued legs.

non_negotiables.requirement: Descending confidence — practice technical descents on narrow roads at speed under fatigue
non_negotiables.why: The Ghisallo descent is nearly 20 km of hairpins on narrow Italian roads. In the wet, it is genuinely dangerous. In the dry, it is where time is gained or lost. Practice descending on tired legs — after a hard climb effort, not when fresh. Focus on braking technique, line selection through hairpins, and body position at speed. If October weather brings rain, your descending skills become the difference between finishing safely and not finishing at all.

videos.title: Giro di Lombardia, la Gran Fondo intitolata a Gimondi
videos.description:
Presentata a Palazzo Lombardia la Gran Fondo intitolata a Felice Gimondi, campionissimo del ciclismo, che si correrà il 13 ottobre con partenza e arrivo a Cantù, in provincia di Como.
Il giorno prima sarà disputato il Giro di Lombardia, ultima classica Monumento dell’anno.
L’assessore alla cultura di Regione Lombardia Francesca Caruso: “Il Lombardia, con la sua storia e la sua tradizione, regala visibilità ai magnifici luoghi del nostro territorio”.
Ai microfoni di Lombardia Notizie anche il leggendario Damiano Cunego, 3 volte vincitore del Giro di Lombardia

www.lombardianotizie.online

videos.title: Gran Fondo Il Lombardia Felice Gimondi 2024 | Best Of
videos.description: Oltre 2000 amatori provenienti da tutto il mondo hanno pedalato il 13 ottobre 2024 sulle orme dei grandi campioni che ieri hanno dato spettacolo, scalando salite mitiche come Ghisallo e Sormano. Si è chiuso così un grande weekend targato RCS Sports & Events.
tags: gran fondo il lombardia felice gimondi
videos.curation_reason: Recent official race highlights showing the scale and international participation of the 2024 event.

videos.title: GRAN FONDO IL LOMBARDIA FELICE GIMONDI A FECCHIO
videos.description:
Il percorso in breve
Partenza da Cantù con i primi 25 km che sono un “mangia e bevi” costante per le strade della Brianza, sino a Maglio, dove incominciano i 7 km più terribili ed emozionanti della gara che porteranno gli atleti sino alla collina di Sormano. Gli ultimi due km di questa erta rappresentano l’apice della corsa e sono diventati in breve tempo un monumento del ciclismo mondiale: il MURO DI SORMANO. Qui, la strada sale vertiginosamente ad una pendenza media del 15,8% ed una massima del 27%. Le scritte sull’asfalto ed i cartelli a bordo strada si susseguiranno per far vivere ad ogni granfondista l’esperienza di un vero professionista. Per i meno allenati è confermata una deviazione di 2km che permette di raggiungere la vetta percorrendo una salita meno impegnativa. A seguire discesa in picchiata fino a Nesso, e poi di nuovo e collina sino a Regatola, dove incomincerà la seconda salita di giornata: il GHISALLO. Le pendenze sono importanti, ma non aggressive come Sormano. La salita è pedalabile ed il paesaggio godibile. Chi vorrà “fare la gara”, potrà spingere rapporti importanti. La pendenza media nei 9km è del 6.2%, e solo verso la fine tocca la punta massima del 14%. In cima, la Madonna del Ghisallo con le sue famose campane ed il museo più celebre della storia del ciclismo vi daranno il benvenuto. Arrivati sul Ghisallo la Gran Fondo è al km 70. Quasi 2/3 di gara sono stati percorsi, e le difficoltà maggiori sono ormai alle spalle.

Ultimi Km
Una lunga (quasi 20 km) e larga discesa fino a Merone permette di recuperare un po’ le forze prima degli ultimi 20 km che ripropongono strappi e salitelle prima di arrivare nel centro di Cantù.
videos.curation_reason: Detailed first-person race content with specific course breakdown including the notorious Muro di Sormano climb details.

videos.title: Gran Fondo Il Lombardia 2023 | Best Of
videos.description:
Oltre 2.200 amatori alla conquista della Gran Fondo Il Lombardia: 
Salite iconiche come Sormano e Ghisallo hanno fatto da sfondo allo spettacolo dei cicloamatori accorsi da tutto il mondo. L’appuntamento, con partenza e arrivo a Cantù, ha chiuso la stagione degli eventi di ciclismo dedicati agli amatori organizzati da RCS Sports & Events.

Over 2200 amateur cyclists on the iconic roads of the Gran Fondo Il Lombardia: iconic climbs such as Sormano and Ghisallo were the best scenario for the spectacle of cyclists from all over the world. The Gran Fondo Il Lombardia, both starting and finishing in Cantù, ends the series of cycling events dedicated to amateur cyclists, organized by RCS Sports & Events.

videos.title: GRAN FONDO FELICE GIMONDI, partenza di 2.000 cicloamatori da Cantù all'alba.
